creator: crest@rlwinm.de
text:
I incorrectly assumed this to be porting/packaging problem, but found out that this isn't the case. Upstream doesn't have resources to support 3rd party use of libstlink and hadn't considered anyone but them calling into it.

My usecase is a bit unusual. I wrote a tool to (ab-)use the ST-LINK programmer as remote memory access interface between the a host PC running my code (tested on FreeBSD and Linux so far) and a STM32 microcontroller offer a faster replacement to the USART. To accomplish this use a pair of 256 byte ring buffers in the microcontroller SRAM acting as single producer, single consumer queues. The code can transfer about 90k-100k bytes per second (about an order of magnitude faster than a 8N1 115200 baud USART). It also requires no chip specific hardware initialization like enabling buses and clocks, mapping alternate functions. It should work on any STM32 with a Thumb2 capable core. It is intended as an alternative to the USART as console for the Mecrisp Stellaris Forth system, but has already been ported to Zeptoforth.

You can find the code at https://github.com/Crest/swdcom should you want to take a look at it.

I filed a GitHub issue with the stlink upstream and they don't want to spend resources on enabling my use of libstlink, but are open to tested contributions working on all platforms.
